scenario: A team's Timer Cycle uses cron expressions extensively. They wonder if all Camunda 8 versions support cron syntax in Timer events, or if some only support ISO 8601.
question: Cron vs ISO 8601 in Camunda 8 Timer events?

options.text: md:104-106 (verbatim: "you can specify a time cycle using cron expressions. Refer to the CronExpression Tutorial for additional information"). 3 CronExpression (6 fields: seconds minutes hours day-of-month month day-of-week). 3 accepts both ? and as "no specific value", but Camunda's documentation consistently uses *`). Pick by readability for the specific schedule.

Claiming Camunda 8 Timer events support only ISO 8601 and not cron expressions understates the platform's capabilities. 3 CronExpression syntax with six fields (seconds minutes hours day-of-month month day-of-week). md:104-106 explicitly documents cron support with a concrete example, confirming both formats are available to modelers in recent versions.

0 standard timer format and has been supported since the earliest Camunda 8 versions. It covers three timer types: Date (absolute moment), Duration (relative delay like PT4H), and Cycle (repeating interval like R5/PT1H). Cron was added as an additional supported format. Teams that need relative durations or absolute single-fire dates must use ISO 8601, which makes ISO 8601 foundational to Timer Event support.

Claiming that neither ISO 8601 nor cron is supported in Camunda 8 Timer events contradicts documented functionality. Timer Events are a core, well-tested feature of Zeebe and Camunda 8. 3 CronExpression are supported. Timer Start Events, Timer Intermediate Catch Events, and Timer Boundary Events all leverage these formats for scheduling process executions, implementing deadlines, and creating repeating triggers in production workflows.

a.text: Correct. Camunda 8 Timer events typically support both formats in recent versions: ISO 8601 (BPMN-spec): Date: absolute moment, 2026-12-31T23:59:59Z. Duration: relative, PT4H (4 hours), P7D (7 days). Cycle: repeating, R5/PT1H (5 times every hour), R/PT24H (unbounded daily). Cron (Spring 5.3, 6 fields with seconds): Format: seconds minutes hours day-of-month month day-of-week. Camunda's verbatim doc example (timer-events.md:106): 0 0 9-17 MON-FRI -- every hour on the hour 9-5 PM UTC, Monday-Friday. Note for day-of-month (idiomatic Camunda form). Other valid forms: 0 0 18 FRI: every Friday at 18:00 (idiomatic ). 0 30 14 1 : 1st of every month at 14:30. 0 0 0 SUN: every Sunday at midnight. Spring 5.3 also accepts ? as "no specific value" (e.g., 0 0 18 ? FRI), but Camunda's docs consistently use -- prefer * for alignment with Camunda's idiom. Convenient for human-meaningful schedules. When to use which: ISO 8601 Duration / Cycle: relative timing, well-defined intervals. Cron: calendar-specific scheduling (specific days, business hours, etc.). ISO 8601 Date: absolute one-shot moment. Choose by which is more readable / maintainable for the specific schedule. Some teams standardise on one; others use both pragmatically.
